sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** – no documented process for handling disputes, injuries, dissatisfaction, or safeguarding concerns; critical for a women's-only space
- **Churn analysis & win-back campaigns** – no SOP linking cancellation reasons to targeted re-engagement (separate from generic marketing)
- **Coach performance review & feedback cycle** – no formal cadence for 1-to-1s, capability gaps, or underperformance conversations
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no documented monthly close, payment processing checks, or fee variance investigation
- **Health & safety incident reporting** – no post-incident protocol (injury documentation, follow-up, insurance notification)
- **New member onboarding beyond sign-up** – no documented first 14-day touchpoint sequence or drop-off recovery
- **Capacity & class scheduling optimization** – no process for reviewing utilization data and adjusting timetables
- **Equipment maintenance & replacement** – no preventive maintenance schedule or failure protocol

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding** – likely missing: probation review trigger, initial feedback at 30/60/90 days, brand voice/tone standards for messaging members
- **Class cancellation protocol** – probably no clear escalation for last-minute coach absence or facility issues
- **Member communication templates** – risk of inconsistent voice across automated emails; needs tone/brand guidelines
- **Pricing & membership tiers** – no documented rules for promos, freezes, downgrades, or refund edge cases
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – the 2 missing (newsletter, blog) suggest no editorial calendar or content approval process either

---

# **Build next**

1. **Member Complaint & Incident Log** – single template covering: complaint type (service/coach/facility/safety

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## INCREASE REVENUE
- **Pricing power is being left on the table.** AR/M has collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop. You've grown members 18% (124→147) but revenue is flat. Audit your membership tiers: introduce a premium tier (small-group training, nutrition plan, unlimited classes vs capped) and migrate 20–30% of your 147 members upward. Target: restore AR/M to £160+ immediately.
- **Churn spiked to 8.33% in April (relocation) and is stuck elevated at 5.74% (May) and 0.00% (June is anomalous — verify data).** Q2 average is 4.69% vs Q1 average of 4.01%. Run exit surveys now; most gyms leak 15–20% when they move. Implement a 30-day post-relocation retention blitz: complimentary PT session, class passes for friends, ambassador referral bonus. Every 1% churn costs ~£1,500/month in repeat revenue.
- **Quiet class utilization is hidden in your data.** You've added 62 members in the new space but Q2 revenue (May+June = £37.9k) trails Q1 (Feb+March = £32.0k, excluding April). The bigger studio should pull more daytime/lunchtime revenue. Introduce 06:00 and 13:00 classes, run a 4-week corporate partnership trial with 3 local employers (£50/month per employee = 30–50 new members fast).

## REDUCE EXPENSES
- **Non-people costs are the crisis.** May–June sit at ~£9.3k/month; Q2 average £9.5k vs Q1 average £7.4k (Jan–Mar: up 28%). April relocation was justified, but June should have stabilized — it hasn't. Breakdown: are utilities/rent doubled with the new space? Insurance? Software? Isolate the £1.8k monthly overspend vs pre-relocation baseline, negotiate rates (rent review, cheaper insurance quote, bundled utility broker). Target: drop non-people costs to £8k/month by August.
- **People costs are creeping: £3,686 in June vs £3,116 in March (+18% in 4 months).** You've added staff for the bigger studio — necessary — but payroll discipline matters. Benchmark: at 147 members, you should

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is over-loaded at 133 sessions; redistribute her PT clients to Eilis and give Eilis target of 20–25 PT slots monthly.** Eilis currently has 11 PTs — she has capacity and should step up here. This reduces JoJo's PT load and frees her for classes.

• **Move Grace's Build: Lower (17 sessions) and Build: Upper (9 sessions) to JoJo; JoJo already owns these formats and is the natural fit.** This is straightforward rotation and removes Grace entirely from class delivery.

• **Assign Grace's Elevate (6 sessions) and Strongevity (3 sessions) to Eilis.** These are lower-intensity formats suited to developing coaches; Eilis needs more class variety and these slots build her confidence outside Tuesdays.

• **Pass Grace's No Sweat Intro (3 sessions) to Eilis as well** — entry-level onboarding isn't high-skill and Eilis should own member first experiences.

• **Treat all InBody scans (41 total) as admin rotation across both active coaches, not coaching load** — stop counting them against PT hours. This frees ~6 hours of fake "load" from the timetable.

• **Outcome: JoJo ~115 sessions (manageable), Eilis ~65 sessions (growing her role), Grace 0 delivery, and no single coach owns any format alone.**

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time:**

1. **Add a paid 30-min "Open Barbell" slot on Monday and Thursday evenings** (your two quietest days) between PT blocks — charge £8-12 per person for structured lift access with coaching cues. This captures members already in the gym and fills dead time without cannibalizing existing classes.

2. **Run a 45-min "Basics Workshop" series on Saturday mornings** (your quietest day, lowest attendance) — rotate topics like "Deadlift Fundamentals," "Squat Setup," "How to Read Your Plate" — free for members, positioned as skill-building that feeds into your Build and Plate classes.

**For quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move "Engine" (52 attendees) from its current slot to Tuesday or Wednesday at lunch or early evening** — your busiest days. Reposition it as a 20-min finisher or cardio burst to appeal to members already coming for Build classes, and promote it hard to Upper/Lower/Full Body attendees as a conditioning add-on. If timing isn't flexible, send targeted invites to recent Build class regulars with "try a free Engine class this week" to test demand.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Audit first, then design**
Before writing anything, spend 2 hours interviewing 3-4 of your best coaches and 5-6 loyal members separately. Ask: "What do you think we offer?" You'll find gaps and confusion. Use their language—not marketing speak—in your final menu. This becomes your reality check before committing to pricing.

**2. Price by tier, not confusion**
Create 3 tiers (entry/mid/premium) rather than à la carte. Coach-facing menu should show margins and package profitability. Member-facing menu should highlight *what's included per tier* (e.g., "unlimited classes + 1 coach session/month" vs. "4 classes + 2 sessions"). Make bundling obvious so you stop leaving money on the table with confused à la carte buyers.

**3. Test pricing on real people**
Don't guess. Show 2-3 mock versions to 10 members next week—ask which feels fair for the value. You'll know immediately if you're too high or leaving cash behind. Use Typeform or simple printed cards; this takes 30 minutes, not a month.

**4. Coach version = accountability tool**
Their menu should include: what each service includes, upsell triggers ("when client X, suggest Y"), and what requires your approval. This prevents coaches underselling and keeps pricing consistent.

**5. Live it by Friday**
Pick your strongest tier and two price points. Email members the menu by EOW. You can refine based on real questions—don't wait for perfection.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

**Immediate execution (this week):**

• **Audit your current drop-off points** — Use Google Analytics to see where visitors bail (homepage? pricing? class details?). That's where confusion lives. Focus refinement there first, not everywhere.

• **Record 5-10 member discovery calls** — Ask new members: "What almost stopped you joining?" and "What question did you have to hunt for?" This beats guessing. Document exact phrases they use.

• **Create a simple FAQ section above the fold** — Not a generic FAQ. Pull from your actual member questions: "Do I need to be fit already?" "What if I've never done weights?" "Is it judgmental?" These are trust-killers for women new to gym culture.

• **Test one high-friction page with new copy** — Pick your weakest converter (likely pricing or "what's included"). Rewrite it using member language from your calls, not fitness jargon. A/B test for 2 weeks before rolling to others.

• **Add a 30-second video of the actual gym** — Not a polished ad. Real women working out, real studio shots. Removes the mystery of "what's it actually like in there?" More conversions than any copy tweak.

**What kills women's gym funnels:** vagueness about community feel, fitness level required, and intimidation factor. Address these three specifically.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Monthly Feedback System - Execution Playbook for Grace

• **Start stupidly small: Post-class QR code scan** — Don't overthink this. Print QR codes on A5 cards by each exit leading to a 3-question Google Form: "What was great?" / "What frustrated you?" / "One thing to fix?" Takes 60 seconds. Track responses in a shared sheet you check weekly. This beats a fancy system you'll abandon.

• **Make it a ritual, not a chore** — Tie feedback collection to your quietest class (e.g., 6pm Tuesday). Have one staff member own it as part of their role (5 mins per session). Make *responding* to feedback visible: pin the top suggestion of the month in the lobby with your action taken. Members who see their input matters will keep giving it.

• **Close the loop visibly or lose momentum** — This is where most gyms fail. Within 2 weeks of collecting feedback, communicate back: "5 members asked for longer showers—we're staggering timeslots." Screenshot wins and post in your member WhatsApp/app. One ignored suggestion kills your next 3 months of honest responses.

• **Harvest your gold nuggets monthly** — Dedicate 30 mins every first Monday: review all feedback, rank by frequency, pick ONE thing to fix that month. Document it (even if just a note). This prevents overwhelm and keeps the system lean.

• **Bypass forms for real insight** — While the form runs, *you* personally do 2-3 post-class check-ins weekly. "How'd that feel?" beats any survey. Spot the quiet members—they never fill forms but leave

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOPs for The Forge: Execution Strategy

**Start with the revenue-driver first:**
Write the personal training SOP before anything else. It's your highest-margin service and the one where inconsistency costs you most (missed sessions, unclear progression, client confusion = churn). Get this bulletproof, then use it as your template for the others.

**Template structure that actually works:**
For each SOP, use: [Client Journey Stage] → [Specific Action/Responsibility] → [Decision Point] → [Outcome]. Example: "New PT member onboarding" → "Assessment completed by Day 3" → "Is she progressing or plateauing?" → "Adjust program or escalate." This prevents the vague "member does stuff" problem.

**Assign ownership NOW, not later:**
Don't write these solo in spreadsheets. Assign each SOP to the person who actually runs it (PT coordinator owns PT SOP, whoever manages fuel forward owns that one). They write a rough draft in 2 hours; you tighten it in 30 mins. Faster, more accurate, and they own the process.

**Digitize immediately—don't let them live in Word:**
Use a simple shared doc or low-code tool (even Notion) where each SOP is a checklist members can access. If it's not visible to staff and members, it becomes a shelf-ware document. Link it in your onboarding emails and gym app.

**Measure compliance within 4 weeks:**
Track whether each SOP is actually being followed (spot-check 5 members per service). If compliance is below 80%, the SOP is either unclear or not being enforced—fix immediately,

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Setup for The Forge

**Start with a content batching sprint (this week):**
- Block 2 hours on one day to film 12-16 short videos at once: members mid-workout, transformation moments, quick tips, behind-the-scenes. Shoot vertically for Reels/TikTok. This kills the "I don't have time" bottleneck that kills most gym owners' consistency.

**Use a free template + automation, not another app:**
- Grab a Google Sheets template (search "content calendar template") or use Canva's free calendar feature—Grace likely already uses Canva. Schedule posts to Instagram/Facebook using Meta Business Suite (free, built-in). This removes daily decision-making and "what should I post?" paralysis.

**Anchor posts to 3 repeating content pillars (3-4 per week):**
- **Member wins** (transformations, testimonials, milestone celebrations—2x/week): drives urgency, social proof, conversion
- **Quick fitness/mindset value** (30-60 sec tips, form checks, motivation—1x/week): keeps non-members engaged, builds authority
- **Behind-the-scenes/community** (class previews, events, staff spotlights—1x/week): humanizes The Forge, builds loyalty

**Assign or outsource the *only* repeatable task:**
- Have one team member (or contractor at £50-100/month) spend 30 mins weekly curating/scheduling posts using your batched content. Grace reviews + approves. This locks in consistency without her living on social media.

**Track one metric ruthlessly:**
- Monitor Instagram/

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Videography Sessions with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW with a 12-month calendar hold** — Book all 6 sessions (8-week intervals) with Jordan this week, not one at a time. This removes scheduling friction later and signals to Jordan you're a reliable client. Use a shared Google Calendar invite so neither of you can flake.

• **Assign a specific "content lead" day before each shoot** — Have Jordan arrive to film during your busiest class time (likely early morning or evening). Pre-brief him on 2-3 specific stories that week (new member wins, transformation, class vibe) so he's not shooting blind. This turns raw footage into usable content, not hours of filler.

• **Create a simple shot list template** — Before each session, send Jordan a one-pager: "We need 30 secs of kettlebell class energy, 2-3 member testimonials (60 secs each), one wide-angle studio shot." Takes you 5 minutes; saves him guesswork and gives you footage you'll actually post.

• **Batch your content calendar around these sessions** — Plan your Instagram/TikTok content for the 8 weeks *after* each shoot. This way you're not scrambling to edit and post—you're working backwards from scheduled content drops to the filming day.

• **Set a micro-budget for B-roll cleanup** — Allocate £100-150 per session for basic editing (cuts, captions, music). Post-production kills momentum; pay Jordan or a student editor to deliver 3-4 polished 15-30 second clips within 2 weeks of filming.

What's your current biggest bottlen

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Automating 6-Week Trial Enquiries at The Forge

• **Audit your current funnel first** — Map exactly where trial enquiries come in (website form, Instagram DM, WhatsApp, phone calls?) and where they drop off. You likely have 3-4 entry points bleeding enquiries. Pick the top 2 and automate those first. Don't over-engineer.

• **Set up a dead-simple welcome sequence** — Use Zapier or Make.com to trigger: (1) instant confirmation email with trial dates/times + parking/what-to-bring, (2) SMS 48hrs before first session, (3) one post-session follow-up asking for feedback. This removes the manual "remember to email them back" friction that kills conversions.

• **Create a pre-trial qualification gate** — Add 2-3 questions to your signup form (fitness experience level, main goal, injury concerns). Route answers to different email templates so your team spends time on serious prospects, not tire-kickers. Saves 3-4 hours per week.

• **Segment by conversion point** — Trials from referrals vs. Instagram ads vs. Google vs. walk-ins behave differently. Automate different messaging for each. Referrals might need less onboarding; cold Instagram prospects need more reassurance they'll fit in.

• **Set a 2-week review checkpoint** — After 2 weeks, pull data: enquiry-to-trial conversion rate, no-show rate, trial-to-member rate. That tells you whether this automation is actually working or just creating noise.

**Start this week: Just do steps 1 + 2. That's

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Based Weekly Checklists + KPIs for The Forge

**1. Start with their actual job outputs, not job titles**
Don't create checklists around "duties." Ask JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah: "What does a perfect week look like for you?" Capture what they *actually do* that moves revenue/retention. Then reverse-engineer 3-4 KPIs per person tied directly to gym growth (e.g., if JoJo does sales, her KPI isn't "conduct consultations"—it's "convert 70% of consultations to memberships").

**2. Make KPIs theirs to own, not Grace's to monitor**
Each person gets 1 primary KPI + 2 supporting ones. JoJo might own: Membership conversions (primary) → New inquiries sourced (supporting) → Member retention rate in her onboarded cohort (supporting). Write it so they *choose* weekly tactics to hit it, not task-checked boxes.

**3. Weekly checklist = 5-7 specific actions, measurable daily/weekly**
Example for retention (if Eilis owns it):
- Contact 3 at-risk members (track: yes/no + outcome)
- Update retention dashboard by Friday EOD
- Deliver 2 one-on-one check-ins with low-attendance members
- Report weekly retention %, compare to previous week

**4. Document in a shared living doc (not PDF), updated Fridays**
Grace reviews Friday afternoon. Each person adds: what they hit, what blocked them, and 1 adjustment for next week. This becomes your growth intelligence—not a filing cabinet exercise

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

• **Pre-session prep (do this 48 hours before):** Send each team member a one-pager with YOUR SSO, the 90-day plan visual, and a blank "My Lane" template asking them to draft their role. This kills vague discussion and surfaces misalignment before the meeting starts.

• **Open with the why, not the what:** Start by telling them exactly why you're running The Forge this way (member retention, revenue target, whatever drives you). Team buys into vision when they understand *your* stakes, not just the plan.

• **Use the "lane clarity" framework in real-time:** Walk through each person's role and ask *them* to call out one specific decision they own vs. one they need your input on. Write it down. This prevents the "I thought you were handling that" problem.

• **Make SSO + 90-day plan *one* visual:** Don't present them separately—show how the quarterly wins ladder into the bigger mission. Team retention drops when they see 90 days as isolated tasks, not progress toward something bigger.

• **Close with accountability, not motivation:** End with: "Here's what success looks like for your lane by Day 30" (not Day 90). Give them a small, measurable win they can own. Momentum kills ambiguity.

What's your current team size? That changes whether this is one 60-min session or staggered one-on-ones.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing — Action Plan for Grace

• **Map actual hours NOW, not roles** — Have each team member log their week (today) in 15-min blocks across: floor supervision, class instruction, admin, cleaning, member check-ins, sales/onboarding. You'll spot where time actually vanishes. Generic job descriptions lie.

• **Identify the 2-3 bottlenecks killing you** — Look for tasks only one person does (member billing? class scheduling? new member inductions?). These create your over-stretched people AND your single points of failure. Prioritize moving these first.

• **Create a "quick win rebalance" in week 1** — Pick ONE admin/operational task currently choking someone out (likely your strongest person) and either delegate it down, automate it, or kill it entirely. Small visible shift = buy-in from staff for bigger changes.

• **Link rebalancing to revenue, not fairness** — Frame moves around "Which tasks directly earn us money or keep members?" Shift low-revenue work (excessive cleaning detail, over-managing trivial admin) away from your revenue-generating staff (instructors, salespeople). This feels logical to the team, not arbitrary.

• **30-day check: measure utilization on THREE metrics only** — Late arrivals/early departures, member complaints dropped, and actual class attendance (if instructor morale improves, people stay longer). Skip the survey; watch behavior.

What's your hunch about who's drowning right now?

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Immediate audit (do this week):**
- Pull last 3 months of bank/card statements. Segment spend: utilities, staffing, supplies, rent, tech. One category will likely be 40%+ of costs—attack that first. Most gyms hemorrhage money on utilities (heating, lighting) or overstaffed off-peak hours.

**Quick wins (implement in 2-3 weeks):**
- **Renegotiate supplier contracts now**—equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, towel services. You have leverage: tell them you're comparing 2-3 alternatives. Even 8-12% reductions are common. Get 3 quotes, use them as negotiation fuel.
- **Right-size staffing to member usage patterns**—map when classes actually fill vs. when you're paying someone to stand around. Cut 1-2 shifts weekly if off-peak attendance is under 60% capacity.

**Structural play (30-60 days):**
- Switch to LED lighting on a schedule (motion sensors in bathrooms/changing rooms). Typical 25-30% utility cut. ROI pays back in 8-10 months.
- Audit your software stack—recurring subscriptions you forgot about, duplicate tools, premium tiers you don't need. Most businesses find £200-500/month in waste here.

**Track obsessively:**
- Set a spreadsheet with baseline spend vs. target (10% reduction). Update weekly. You'll spot what's actually moveable vs. fixed costs—that's where real decisions happen.

What's your biggest expense category right now?

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — How to Execute

• **Set a fixed calendar slot (same day/time each month)** — Grace audits this herself before passing to accountant. Tuesday morning post-close works best. Non-negotiable 90-min block stops it slipping and compounds month-on-month tracking accuracy.

• **Track 5 metrics only, not 20** — Focus on: (1) Monthly Recurring Revenue by membership tier, (2) Cash burn rate, (3) Member acquisition cost vs lifetime value ratio, (4) Class utilization rates by time slot, (5) Churn rate. Anything else dilutes action.

• **Create a one-page dashboard template in Excel/Sheets** — Same format every month. Color-code red/amber/green targets. Takes 20 mins to populate once structure exists. Shared with your accountant so they know what matters to you.

• **Cross-reference against last month immediately** — Don't just log numbers. Spot the 1-2 weird variances in 10 mins (e.g., "6 PM class attendance dropped 12%"). This triggers next actions, not reports that sit unread.

• **Use the 5-minute debrief with yourself** — What moved positively? One thing to double down on next month. What tanked? One conversation to have (e.g., with your PT team about class scheduling). Document it. This turns data into decisions.

**Start this month.** Don't wait for "perfect" systems.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Start with 24 months of actual data (not estimates).** Pull your last 2 years of membership numbers, churn rates, class attendance, and retail sales week-by-week. This is your baseline—guesswork kills forecasts. Use a simple Google Sheet with rows for each revenue stream (memberships, PT, classes, retail, corporate packages if you have them).

• **Build three scenarios: conservative, realistic, optimistic.** For each, assume different membership growth rates (be honest—what's realistic for Belfast right now?), seasonal dips (January surge, summer slump), and cost inflation. This forces you to stress-test assumptions rather than pray for one outcome.

• **Model your unit economics first.** Calculate: cost per new member acquisition → lifetime value of that member → break-even point. If acquiring someone costs £150 but they stay 8 months at £35/month = £280 profit, that's your growth math. Use this to justify marketing spend decisions.

• **Set monthly cash-flow tracking (not just profit).** When do you actually pay rent, staff, suppliers? Project 12 months ahead showing closing balance each month. This reveals if you're profitable but cash-strapped—the killer for gyms.

• **Link forecasting to one decision you need to make NOW.** Are you hiring a PT? Launching a new class? Running a campaign? Run the forecast against that decision: "If I spend £2k on ads in March, what membership ROI do I need to justify it?" This makes the numbers purposeful, not just an exercise.

What's your current biggest cash-flow pressure point?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Build Your Service P&L — Here's the Exact Move

**1. Start with your three buckets of revenue TODAY**
Pull last month's actual numbers: total class revenue, total PT revenue, total add-on revenue (merchandise, nutrition plans, supplements). Don't overthink it—you need rough accuracy, not perfection. If your booking system doesn't break this down, manually categorize your last 50 transactions.

**2. Assign costs using a simple allocation rule (not perfect, but fast)**
For each service bucket, calculate: direct staff cost (instructors/PT rates) + allocated facility cost (% of rent/utilities based on what % of your floor space/hours it uses). For add-ons, just track COGS (what you paid for stock) + packaging. You'll know within 80% accuracy which is actually profitable—that's enough to act on.

**3. Calculate unit economics—revenue per transaction, profit per transaction**
For classes: revenue per class ÷ avg attendance = per-person margin. For PT: per-session revenue minus trainer cost. For add-ons: per-item gross margin. This tells you *which service makes real money*, not which looks busy.

**4. Spot the obvious win in your next 48 hours**
Gut check: which service likely has 40%+ margin? Double down on *selling more of that*. If classes are thin margin but PT is fat, your marketing should shift. If add-ons are untapped, train your front desk to upsell them on every transaction.

**5. Lock in one metric to track weekly**
Pick one—gross margin % by service or profit per class hour. Track it. Habits beat spreadsh

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations — The Forge Edition

• **Run a live test NOW, not in theory**: Pick 3 fake email addresses and submit enquiries through your website today. Track every touchpoint—what emails arrive, when, in what order, from which platform (Mailchimp? Zapier? native gym software?). You'll spot duplicates immediately when test emails land twice. This takes 30 mins and beats any manual audit.

• **Map your actual tech stack on paper**: List every tool that touches enquiry-to-trial (website form → email → booking system → SMS reminders → trial confirmation). You likely have overlapping automation layers you've forgotten about. Check if Zapier, your CRM, and your booking software are all trying to send the same "welcome" email.

• **Kill the worst offender first**: Don't fix everything. Find the automation causing the most obvious friction (e.g., trial reminders going out twice, or enquiries dropping into a dead email inbox). Fix that one today. One win beats perfect planning.

• **Assign a 20-minute weekly check**: Have one person (ideally your admin/reception) spot-check that 5 random new enquiries hit trial booking within your target window. Automations drift—a 5-min weekly sweep beats another full audit in 6 months.

• **Document the working flow visually**: Screenshot the actual sequence—even on Miro or a whiteboard photo. Grace needs to *see* it to explain gaps to staff. This also makes it easy to brief a VA if you hire one later.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Execution

**Identify your 3 tasks FIRST (find the pain, not the fancy):**
- **Membership inquiry responses** — Grace or team manually answering "Do you offer trial classes?" / "What's your pricing?" via Instagram/email daily. Use Tidio or ManyChat to auto-reply with class schedule + trial booking link within 60 seconds of inquiry.
- **Class cancellation notifications + rescheduling** — When someone cancels, you're manually texting the waitlist. Build a Zapier workflow: cancellation → automatic SMS to 5-person waitlist with rebook link (takes 30 mins to set up once).
- **Monthly billing reminders + churn recovery** — Manual emails to members with expiring memberships. Set Stripe → email automation to send "your membership expires in 7 days" + incentive code ($20 off renewal) automatically.

**Build it in this order (not overthinking):**
1. **Pick task #1 this week** — the one costing Grace 5+ hours/month. Spend 2 hours setting up (not researching, building). Zapier + Stripe takes 45 mins if you've done it before.
2. **Measure the actual win** — track time saved and response speed. Screenshot before/after. This proves ROI to justify task #2.
3. **Task #2 next week** — momentum builds when you see the first one work.

**Reality check:** Don't try to "optimize everything." The real win is reclaiming 10-15 hours/month so Grace can focus on member retention conversations and referral partnerships — the stuff

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Install SSL certificate immediately** (if not done) — use Let's Encrypt (free) or your hosting provider's one-click install. Non-negotiable for payment pages and member logins. Takes 30 mins, kills trust issues instantly.

• **Run a free security audit today** — use Sucuri or Wordfence (if WordPress). This identifies your actual vulnerabilities in 10 minutes so you're not guessing. Screenshot the results to show members you're serious if you ever market this.

• **Lock down member data access** — enforce strong password requirements (12+ characters), enable two-factor authentication for admin accounts, and limit who can access payment/membership records. One careless team member = massive liability.

• **Set up automated backups to external storage** — configure daily backups (Backwpup plugin or hosting-level) stored off-site. A ransomware hit mid-launch kills momentum. This is your insurance policy, not optional.

• **Add a simple privacy policy page linking to your data handling practices** — most small gyms skip this. Spend 30 mins customizing a template (Termly or iubenda) and publish it. Covers you legally when members sign up online, especially in the UK where GDPR matters.

**Real talk:** Grace, don't outsource this to someone "tech-savvy." Get professional security advice (even a one-off £200 audit) — one breach loses members faster than you gained them.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

1. **You've underinvested into a collapsing funnel.** CPL has quadrupled from £6 to £27 since April; lead volume is down 94%. July's £109 spend is a tenth of April's. This isn't efficiency—it's starvation. Your close rate (75%) is actually solid, so the problem is lead generation, not sales.

2. **Creative fatigue and audience saturation are likely culprits.** Four months of the same ads to the same women in Belfast (a small market) will tank performance. Your audience has seen everything; you're now paying premium rates to reach people who've already scrolled past you twice.

3. **July's profitability (£458) is a mirage.** You're making money on scraps because you've pulled budget. Scale this spend to April levels (£435) at July's CPL (£27) and you'd get ~16 leads, ~12 sales, ~£3,100 revenue, but spend £432—not profitable at that ratio. You need to fix the mechanics first.

---

**Actions**

1. **Refresh creative immediately—test three new angles this week.** Pull your last 30 days of ad creative. You need: (a) a testimonial-led video (member transformation, 15–20 sec, shot on phone), (b) a pain-point angle ("tired of mixed gyms? Here's why women-only changes everything"), (c) a FOMO/urgency hook ("6-week trial spots filling up—only X left this month"). Run each at £30/week to a cold audience.

2. **Segment your audience ruthlessly.** Stop targeting "women interested in fitness" across all of Belfast. Split into: (a) never engaged (cold traffic), (b) visited website but didn't convert, (c) engaged with ads but didn't click. Remarket to (c) with a different offer ("No commitment—first session free"). Dial back spend on (a) until creative improves.

3. **Rebuild your audience and test lower CPL channels.** Your April £6 CPL suggests you had a working playbook then. Use lookalike audiences built from your March
